why: A is accurate and faithful, correctly framing automap as routing-based self-IP selection vs a pool of explicit addresses, while B's 'unique source IP per connection' and C's route-domain emphasis are slightly off.
C and B stay grounded to the AFM guide content while A appends a constructed techdocs URL not in the reference; C is the cleanest and most faithful.

A and C correctly explain route-domain isolation and cite the in-reference multiple-route-domains article, while B misattributes the answer to K9655 (a GTM auto-discovery article) with a constructed link.
C correctly uses the real sideband command set (connect/send/recv/close) with examples and ties in the MAC-masquerade and memory-leak bugs from the reference, most complete though somewhat long.
All correctly report ES restart symptom, NoClassDefFoundError, doc-limit condition, and 8.0.0.1 fix; C is complete and faithful while A adds a small unsourced HTTP-module example.
A and B accurately distinguish automatic self-IP selection from an explicit pool from real knowledge; A is the most thorough and B the most concise, with C marginally looser.
All three give accurate, real AFM DoS/protocol/DNS capabilities; A balances coverage and concision while B is the most complete but notably padded and repetitive.
All correctly explain route domains as isolated routing tables enabling overlapping IPs; B is most complete with a concrete same-IP/different-domain example.

All fabricate iRule syntax, but B is closest with connect/send semantics whereas A invents a 'db sideband set'/TCP::client_open scheme and C invents a nonexistent 'sideband -create/-send/-close' command set.
A and B honestly admit they lack the bug details without inventing anything, while C falsely asserts the ID 'is not a real bug identifier' (it is real), making its refusal slightly less accurate.
